ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken mole; mapped to broader chicken dish class FOODON_00002764.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken mole; A0CKN (chicken meat dish) used as the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken mole dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken mole is approximately 200 g, representing one cup of braised chicken pieces coated in mole sauce, as commonly plated at a restaurant or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Mole — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Chicken Mole (cr_id G00010811) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken mole (NDB No. 18393 / closest analogue) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate chicken-based mixed dish with chili and spice sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken mole in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the analogous category of low-carbohydrate chicken-based mixed dishes with chili sauce (carbohydrate 3.22 g/100g, predominantly from dried chili, tomato, and cocoa). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (25 × 3.22 × 2) / 100 = 1.6 g, classified as very low. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 542 mg/100 g — borderline-high; partial offset from potassium 232 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sodium preparation using low-sodium chicken broth, as the 542 mg/100 g sodium content approaches the high-concern threshold of 600 mg/100 g despite partial mitigation from potassium at 232 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 3.22 g/100 g, low sugar 2.12 g/100 g, high protein 18.97 g/100 g; estimated GI 25 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken mole is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.22 g/100 g), estimated low GI of 25, and high protein (18.97 g/100 g) which supports glycemic stability; however, if served with white rice or tortillas, the overall meal glycemic load increases substantially and starch portions should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 542 mg/100 g is the primary concern; saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100 g is low; cholesterol 78 mg/100 g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the moderate sodium content (542 mg/100 g) is the primary concern and reduced-sodium preparation is recommended; the low saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g), absence of trans fat, and anti-inflammatory polyphenols from cocoa and spices in the mole sauce are cardioprotective factors that partially offset the sodium risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, cumin, clove), cocoa polyphenols, low saturated fat; nightshade content is a caution for sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken mole is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and low saturated fat, but nightshade-sensitive individuals should request a nightshade-free mole variant as tomato and chili peppers contain solanine which may aggravate symptoms in susceptible patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Mole is a Mexican braised chicken dish cooked in mole sauce — a complex, deeply flavoured sauce made from dried chili peppers, unsweetened cocoa or dark chocolate, tomato, onion, garlic, and a blend of spices including cinnamon, cumin, clove, and black pepper, thickened with sesame seeds and peanuts. The dish has a rich, earthy, mildly spicy, and subtly bittersweet flavour profile with a velvety sauce texture coating tender braised chicken pieces. Per 100 g, it provides 137 kcal, 18.97 g protein, 5.58 g fat (saturated fat approximately 1.5 g), and only 3.22 g carbohydrate with 2.12 g sugar and 0.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 542 mg/100 g, which is borderline-high and the primary clinical concern. The dish is clinically notable for its very low glycemic index (estimated GI 25), high protein content supporting satiety and glycemic stability, and the anti-inflammatory polyphenol content from cocoa and spices. It is suitable for type 2 diabetes management and generally favourable for rheumatoid arthritis, but requires sodium reduction for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Chicken mole is commonly served with rice or tortillas in Mexican cuisine and is found in Singapore restaurants as a Western-international menu item.
common_user_queries: Is chicken mole good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in chicken mole?
Is chicken mole high in calories?
Can I eat chicken mole if I have high blood pressure?
What is mole sauce made of?
Is chicken mole suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010811, confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved GI source as no direct measurement exists for chicken mole.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken with mole sauce (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. Trans fat set to 0.0 g as confirmed negligible for this preparation type per USDA data. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for chicken mole are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010811, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue with complete micronutrient data was identified as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken mole exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = 1.6 g (very low). 4. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Western' and ethnic_tags as ['Western']; this has been corrected to 'Mexican' as chicken mole is a canonical Mexican dish. The Singapore region tag is retained as the record originates from the HPB Singapore database. 5. Halal flag set to true as chicken mole prepared with halal-certified chicken and no alcohol-based ingredients is halal; consumers should verify halal certification of commercial mole paste. 6. Allergen note: peanuts and sesame seeds are standard mole ingredients and represent major allergens.
